algorithm_comprehension.notes: Continuous Batching is not a tiled/complex algorithm requiring tiling visualization. Step-by-step numerical trace in Cell 7 with concrete values (B=512, 3 requests) provides excellent walkthrough. Bubble analysis with mathematical derivation and concrete numbers. Restaurant kitchen analogy is effective for intuition. First-time-reader test: passes — reader can explain CB on a whiteboard.

code_walkthrough.notes: Comprehensive code walkthrough in Cell 5 covering all 5 subsections of schedule(): method signature, Phase 1 (running), Phase 2 (waiting), SchedulerOutput construction, preempt logic, and update_after_step. ALL line number ranges verified against the current implementation file: L258-L275 (method sig), L277-L350 (Phase 1), L352-L418 (Phase 2), L421-L434 (output), L436-L460 (preempt), L462-L515 (update). Demo output shown in Cell 5 matches actual python3 run. vLLM diff explanations present after each subsection. A previous review flagged incorrect line numbers — they have been corrected in this version.

issues.description: The REFERENCE comments in the chapter's code blocks use 'scheduler.py:XXX' without 'vllm/' prefix, which is ambiguous — it could refer to either the implementation file or vLLM source. The implementation file establishes the convention in its header, but readers new to the chapter might not know which file is being cited. Consider disambiguating.

chapter_structure.notes: Chapter ID 04-continuous-batching is unique. Content correctly focused on CB (chunked prefill is a section within, not standalone). Cell numbering consistent (Cell 2 through Cell 11, no gaps). Cross-chapter references to Ch3 and Ch5 resolve correctly.

source_grounding.notes: Every Cell (2-7) has at least one explicit vLLM file:line reference. Source Mapping Table in Cell 9 is excellent: 10+ rows covering all major classes and methods. Implementation code has # REFERENCE: comments on every function. Linter passed: 'All grounding checks passed!'. The acid test passes — a reader can open the vLLM repo and find scheduler.py:L352, request.py:L310, kv_cache_manager.py, etc.

issues.description: Cell 3 opens with vLLM source references to scheduler.py:L388-L556 and L568-L846, but the later paragraphs (bubble analysis, formula derivation) rely on the implementation file references (L539-L575, L578-L627) rather than vLLM source. This is acceptable since the implementation mirrors vLLM's design and the Source Trail provides the vLLM entry point.

formula_renderability.notes: One blocking issue found (\text{} on line 102) and flagged by linter. No \boxed{}, \tag*{}, inline \frac, or one-line $$ found. 4 instances of high inline formula density and 4 complex inline formulas (non-blocking warnings).

issues.description: \text{ 步} in formula: '$$T_{\mathrm{static}} = 800 + 100 = 900 \text{ 步}$$'. Must replace \text{} with alternative. Best fix: remove the Chinese character from the formula: '$$T_{\mathrm{static}} = 800 + 100 = 900$$ 步。'
4 paragraphs with 3-6 inline formulas each. Consider promoting some to block formulas for readability.
4 complex inline formulas (37-72 chars) that should be block formulas, e.g., '\sum(P_i + O_i) = (800 + 200 + 50) + (20 + 50 + 100) = 1050 ...'

Cell 3's bubble analysis uses a 3-request workload (800+20, 200+50, 50+100) to calculate 0.26% utilization, then immediately references bubble_analysis() output from an 8-request workload (4x2048+4x128) showing 58.3% utilization. The chapter acknowledges the mismatch ('等等——这里用的是 8 个请求的测试...'), but the sudden shift in both workload size AND utilization metric (GPU throughput vs per-batch-position) could confuse a first-time reader.
The kitchen analogy ('800 道汤 + 20 道主菜', '512 个灶台') is effective and memorable — a highlight of the chapter.

References to Ch5 (memory management) and Ch7 (prefix cache) as deferred topics are accurate per the outline. Ch5 = 06-memory-management (block pool), Ch7 = 08-prefix-cache. All cross-references resolve.

tests_pass.description: All 14 tests pass (14 passed in 0.14s). Chapter correctly reports '14 passed in 0.16s' — minor timing variance is acceptable.

demo_runnable.description: python3 implementation/scheduler.py runs successfully and produces output matching the chapter's annotated trace (Steps 1-3 verified).

diagram_reference.description: Cell 11 references ./diagram_bubble.png — file exists at artifacts/04-continuous-batching/narrative/diagram_bubble.png. SVG source (diagram_bubble.py, .svg) also present.

test_count_mismatch.description: Previous review flagged '12 passed' when there were 14 tests. Current chapter correctly states '14 passed in 0.16s'. Now consistent.

line_number_accuracy.description: Previous review flagged incorrect line numbers. Current chapter's line ranges (L258-L434, L277-L350, L352-L418, L421-L434, L436-L460, L462-L515) have been verified against the implementation file and are correct.

revision_instructions:
One specific, trivial fix needed:

1. **Fix \text{} on line 102** (formula_renderability, blocking): Change:
   ```
   $$T_{\mathrm{static}} = 800 + 100 = 900 \text{ 步}$$
   ```
   to:
   ```
   $$T_{\mathrm{static}} = 800 + 100 = 900$$
   步。
   ```
   (Remove the Chinese character from the formula block; put it outside.)

That is the ONLY blocking issue. All other dimensions pass. This is a 30-second fix. After fixing, run:
```
python3 scripts/lint_formulas.py artifacts/04-continuous-batching/narrative/chapter.md
python3 scripts/lint_source_grounding.py artifacts/04-continuous-batching/
```
Both should pass cleanly.
blocking_issues: Line 102: \text{ 步} in formula — must be replaced (\text{} uses amsmath which may not be loaded in all renderers)
non_blocking_suggestions: Cell 3: The 0.26% and 58.3% utilization numbers measure different things (GPU throughput vs per-batch-position). A brief note explaining why they differ would help first-time readers.
Cell 5: Consider adding a brief forward-reference to Cell 6 for the data structures used in the walkthrough, so readers know what a Request/SchedulerOutput is before seeing the code.
4 complex inline formulas (37-72 chars) could be promoted to block formulas for better rendering.
4 paragraphs with high inline formula density could be reduced.

Cell 11 references 'short request TTFT can be reduced 63x' but this specific claim is not demonstrated with actual numbers in the chapter. Consider adding a brief calculation or removing the claim to avoid overclaiming.
